blocks.content: The summer was fading.
Chilly winds blew in the mornings and evenings, and plants prepared to don their autumn colors.
But the days remained hot, and the students were relentless in their final push.
Only one week remained.
Classes had ended the previous week, leaving only the final exams.
Exams were held sequentially, one class per day, starting from Class Ten.
On the sixth day, Class Five students soothed their pounding hearts as they headed to the exam hall.
Shirone's group picked at breakfast and arrived early.
The exam lasted exactly 12 hours, with only five-minute breaks.
Compared to ordinary schools, this was grueling, but for magic school students, maintaining focus was as crucial as knowledge.
The exams were divided into Types A, B, and C—same questions, different answer arrangements—to prevent cheating and lucky guesses.
Shirone had Type A, Iruki Type B, and Nade Type C.
Assigned to different halls, they stepped into the corridor beforehand to calm their nerves.
Even the troublemakers were tense—their half-year efforts hinged on this.
Nade shivered despite the warm weather.

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: Ugh. I'm dying. Shirone, how about you?

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Feel like puking. One mistake, and it's over. Especially in math.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: Stay calm. We have time. Save the nerves for the exam paper.
At Iruki's words, the two steadied themselves.
But when the starting bell rang, even they paled.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Let's go. See you with smiles later.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: Right. Good luck!
The three parted ways, promising to reunite in 12 hours.
Supernatural Psychic Science Lab.
Shirone's group was busy grading their exams.
Though the papers couldn't be leaked, Iruki had perfectly reconstructed them.
A Servant's memory knew no forgetting.
It was like storing documents in mental drawers, retrievable anytime.
Iruki recited questions, Nade transcribed, and Shirone checked answers.
They worked until dawn to reconstruct all subjects.
Exhausted, they split up to tally scores.

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: Ugh, I'm nervous. Think I bombed a few.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: Same. This is brutal. Even perfect scores in other subjects won't save us if one falls short.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: But that's why we formed the study group. Let's finish grading first.
Hours later, their scores took shape.
Nade was the first to leap up.

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: Yes! 82 average! Cleared 80 in every subject!
No high marks, but balanced scores secured an 82.
The former delinquent, who'd once scoffed at Class Four, now couldn't be happier.
Iruki and Shirone stayed quiet.
True celebration would come only if all three advanced.
Iruki leaned back, wiping sweat.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: Done. 87. Got one 80 in language. One more wrong, and it'd have been over.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Whoa! You passed too? Congrats! Class Four!

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: Whatever. Feels good, though.
Nade and Iruki turned to Shirone.
If he passed, their dream of advancing together would come true.
But Shirone was silent.
Iruki noticed his hand had stopped moving.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: Shirone? What's wrong? Did you fail?

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: What's the matter? What's your score?

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: Whoa, that's amazing!
Nade was genuinely surprised.
They'd aimed for all three to advance, but hearing it felt miraculous.
Shirone, a commoner's son with no academic background, had averaged 86 after just half a year of effort.
Compared to Iruki's 87—flawless in math thanks to his Servant ability—Shirone's desperation was evident.
Iruki tilted his head.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: You… did well, right? Did you bomb a subject?

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: That's the thing—I'm not sure.
Not sure? What do you mean? Explain.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: The last multiple-choice math proof—what was the answer?

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: That? The one linking two equations? The answer was super long.
Iruki's eyes widened.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: Don't tell me…?

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Yeah, I can't remember which proof I picked. I redid it, but it's the same. I was torn between two back then.
What's your score? If you got it wrong, what's your score?
Iruki and Nade grasped the gravity.
The last question was worth 2 points.
One mistake meant advancement or failure.

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: How can you not remember? Which option did you pick?

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Option 3. But I can't check—I had Type A.
Nade snatched the test paper.
The last question's options were twenty lines long—a nightmare.
He'd surely gotten it wrong too. Probably only Iruki had aced it in Class Five.
Shirone clutched his head.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: I thought it was 2 or 3. With 10 seconds left, it hit me, but… I can't remember what it was.
Last-second recalls happened.
Nade and Iruki, having grown up in magic school, understood the feeling.
But this decided advancement.
Failing meant another half-year in Class Five.

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: Damn it! I can't take this!

blocks.speaker: Iruki
blocks.content: What're you gonna do?

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: Check directly. Let's ask Ms. Siena. She's our homeroom teacher.
Shirone couldn't focus either.
The three left the lab and headed to the exam hall.
Grading had taken a full day, so Class Four's exams were now underway.
During lunch, Shirone's group slipped in and waited anxiously for the teachers.
Thirty minutes later, Siena appeared.

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: Teacher! Teacher!

blocks.speaker: Siena
blocks.content: Hmm? Why are you here? Class Five's exams ended yesterday.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: The last multiple-choice math proof in Type A! What's the answer?
Siena tilted her head.
Her specialty was chemistry, but all teachers reviewed exam papers at least three times.
Even non-majors could handle student-level math.

blocks.speaker: Siena
blocks.content: Why the sudden question?

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: Please! It's really important!
Seeing Nade's desperation, Siena recalled the question.
Its difficulty had stuck with her.

blocks.speaker: Siena
blocks.content: Oh, that? For Type A, it's Option 2.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Option 2…
He'd picked Option 3.
His score: 78.
A 2-point difference meant failure.
His breath caught.

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Option 2? How… How could it be Option 2…
Muttering, Shirone suddenly clutched his neck.
Then, he collapsed backward.
Nade barely caught him.

blocks.speaker: Nade
blocks.content: Shirone! You okay? Snap out of it! You're Indestructible Diamond!

blocks.speaker: Shirone
blocks.content: Option 2? How… How could it be Option 2…
As they dragged him away, Shirone kept mumbling.
Siena blinked, then chuckled and headed to the faculty office.

blocks.speaker: Siena
blocks.content: Those kids never run out of energy, do they?
The office buzzed with grading teachers.
Siena sat and began grading.
Then, on a whim, she pulled out the Type A math exam.
The last multiple-choice, was it?
